Flashblocks.wtf.title: Flashblocks? wth?
Flashblocks.wtf.content:
I often read blogs of people i know personally, very few people i know write blogs but the ones that do i find it very interesting to read them. So i read this blog from azfuller. I found it very interesting, was a good refresher of Game of Life, and i learned about flashblocks. I did some quick research into what flashblocks are and its interesting. With flashblocks.. rollups like base can achieve 200ms transaction confirmations.. they use something called as partial blocks to do this.
This got me thinking.. how does this work?  How does partial blocks.. work? do they send out a pending block containing just the transactions included so far and confirmed to be included? If so in that case.. is 200ms confirmation a real thing.. if the block is not completely confirmed.. is it just a way of phrasing it? Well i got to the answers.. pretty quicklyl.. searching up for “Flashblocks” on google didn’t give me much other than the news of base using flashblocks which was trending when i searched.. it saturated the search results.. so then i searched for flashblocks flashbots since i knew it was built by flashbots.. and still didn’t find much, then i discovered from some X posts that its actually called Rollup boost by flashblocks and base is not the first one to use it. It launched with Unichain, uniswaps on chain..  Base was just good at marketing it.. Unichain already got this..
So I started looking into it. and stumbled upon the Rollup boost from flashbots. which is an open source tooling for rollups, I don’ft know much about it yet to clearly describe It in simple words but I think Its some tooling for roll ups based on OP stack to have its own custom modular components. Its a sidecar solution for op stock Chains to accept blocks from external block builders. I think previously the sequence was the one that built all the blocks and there was no other parties involved.. with this Rollup boost side car there can be external block builders which means further decentralization.
One of the major features Of Rollup boost is Verifiable Priority ordering.. The transaction ordering is verifiable and transparent, which is nice. Another word that I saw that was thrown around a lot IS TEE.
Trusted execution environments… Rollup boost uses TEE for this verifiable ordering.. And another big feature of rollup boost is 250ms confirmation time. So from what I read, the block time is actwall I second but the transaction get confirmed much quicker by using partial blocks which are guaranteed to be included.
Unichain was the first one to do this.
While looking up and researching this I touched upon a lot of topics, like types of rollups.-Fraud proots in optimistic rollups etc. And TEE of course. Feel like abearly scratched the surface of these Roulkup world.
Rollup boost was inspired by some technologies in Solana and Celestia.
So rollup boost is a tool mainly to externalize block building outsource block building to another party, maybe a TEE based builder so that we can have VPO - Verifiable priorrity ordering.. In its current stage the transacitons are received by a centralised RPC and then passed on to a TEE based block builder and then passed on to a centralised sequencer, in future iterations when they decentralise the rpc and the sequencer its complete.

How-I-Over-Engineered-This-Blog-Instead-.title: How I Over-Engineered This Blog Instead of Paying $10
How-I-Over-Engineered-This-Blog-Instead-.content:
How I Over-Engineered This Blog Instead of Paying $10
Am I too late to the blogging scene? Probably, yes. Who reads blogs these days, huh? I do, and probably a very small set of people like me do. And mostly, this is for me, not really for others. And i wouldn’t really call this a blog, its more like taking notes in public. Thought it’d be kind of fun to learn in public. And this is the first such session, me trying to setup the perfect free blogging setup instead of paying 10$ for a service which would have done the job much better than me.

A Quest for not paying
Today, I took the time to set up my blogging workflow. I already use Obsidian a lot for note-taking and journaling, so I searched for ways to publish Obsidian notes as blogs. There’s a nice paid tool called Obsidian Publish, Me being me, of course, I’d spend hours of engineering effort to create something instead of paying 10 bucks for it. And I did exactly that.
I found this nice tool called Quartz, a static site generator built with Obsidian in mind. Jumped into the docs and set it up.

The Struggles
Now, I faced a few problems.


Syncing Notes Like a Mad Scientist
I use obsidian-git to sync my notes across multiple devices. Also, because I don’t want to pay for Obsidian Sync (which, to be fair, would have been a much better UX).


Keeping Secrets, Sharing Thoughts
To publish my blogs, I needed Quartz to track only the notes I wanted to go public. This wasn’t very straightforward. What I ended up doing was creating a git subtree called Public and using Quartz there.


GitHub Pages Said “Nope”
GitHub won’t let you use GitHub Pages for free with private repos. So I had to write a GitHub Actions workflow to copy over the build folder generated by Quartz to a new public repo and push it there each time I make a change in my Obsidian repo (which contains all my private notes, hence why I can’t just make it public).


After hours… I managed to get it working. (If you’re reading this, then yeah, it’s working.)

I Did It!
It was fun… kinda. It’s been a while since I’ve been able to spend time on stuff like this, and I’m glad I did. I’m going to start writing more here—more public notes. I don’t care if anyone reads them or not. Just knowing that someone might and that they might find it useful is enough. But also, it feels strangely satisfying to just put my thoughts out there on the open web, accessible to anyone.
Now, I kinda want to revamp my personal website and add more stuff there, but I only have so much time. I’ll leave it for another day.

index.title: Hello 👋🏻
index.content:
Hi, I’m Krishnanand V P, a very curious human being, software engineer, and an imperfect work in progress. I love writing code, and while I sometimes struggle to keep my curiosity in check, it has mostly worked in my favour. I spend most of my days writing code.
Currently, I’m a Software Engineer at Edge & Node, where I work on The Graph. I contribute to graph-node, the software that runs subgraphs—helping developers index and organize data from the blockchain.
So yeah, I’m a nerd who writes code. I’m in love with creating, and almost always thinking, “Yeah, I can build that.” I have hundreds of unfinished projects and another hundred ideas that will probably end up on that list.
Somehow, you ended up in this little corner of the internet where I share my thoughts and dump my brain. Welcome.
